reasoning: The finding misclassifies a stored-body-replay pattern as SSRF. SSRF requires attacker control over the destination URL or host. In run_non_interactive_api_request() (common.php line 484), wp_remote_post is called with a hardcoded URL 'https://realfavicongenerator.net/api/favicon'; only the request body is sourced from the stored option. The body is the value of the 'non_interactive_request' JSON field (api-response.php line 64), obtained verbatim from the upstream API response and stored via set_favicon_configured() (common.php line 232-235) after nonce verification. In cron runs (ACTION_CHECK_FOR_UPDATE), this stored value is retrieved (get_non_interactive_api_request(), line 213) and POSTed back to the same fixed domain (line 450). No URL is derived from attacker-controlled data; PHP/WP wp_remote_post does not interpret the POST body as a redirect target or secondary URL. The attacker (a compromised RFG API) can only influence the favicon generation parameters replayed in subsequent cron runs — this is a supply-chain trust issue affecting favicon content, not a server-side request to an arbitrary destination. The SSRF classification does not apply. Additionally, initial storage of this value requires a legitimate admin-initiated favicon generation flow guarded by a WP nonce (admin.php line 233) and a per-response custom-parameter nonce check (admin.php line 248), so there is no unauthenticated or cross-site write path either.
